patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 32-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly, a bit worried but not dramatic. Open with: “I’ve been getting more and more short of breath when I do normal things.” Early in the visit, also volunteer: fatigue with small chores, heart pounding/racing on stairs, and that a doctor once mentioned a heart murmur.

If asked about specifics, report: lightheadedness on standing/climbing stairs, near-fainting when rushing, exertional chest tightness that improves with rest, ankle swelling by day’s end, needing extra pillows due to shortness of breath lying flat, and occasional bluish lips/fingertips when very winded or cold.

Do not offer unless asked directly: avoiding outings because you can’t keep up, peeing less when legs are puffy, a few pounds gained in weeks, and “thumping” in chest/neck when lying down. Only reveal low sex drive/shortness of breath during sex if asked; be slightly embarrassed.

Express misconceptions: you wondered about asthma, being out of shape/stressed, or reflux after spicy food.

If the clinician shows empathy, thank them and become more open. Ask about costs/insurance, frequent tests, travel to big centers, and whether you can keep working.

Provides safety-net advice for urgent evaluation with any head trauma, severe headache/neurologic symptoms, uncontrolled bleeding, or signs of intracranial hemorrhage.

Assumes older sleep medications like glutethimide are “stronger and therefore better,” and that if they worked for someone years ago they must be appropriate now.
Believes alcohol (a glass or two of wine) is a healthy way to “knock herself out” and doesn’t count as a sleep aid.
Thinks checking her phone in bed helps her relax and that blue light effects are exaggerated.
economic_concerns: Worries that safer or newer treatments will be expensive and not covered, so she prefers “older, cheaper pills.”
Feels she can’t afford time off work for therapy or follow-up visits, and wants a quick prescription solution.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 46-year-old anxious project coordinator. Speak tangentially: jump between work stress, sleep, and worries; need gentle refocusing. Start with: “I just can’t sleep most nights, and it’s starting to mess with everything.” Volunteer: long sleep onset (hours), middle-of-night awakenings, daytime exhaustion/brain fog, increased irritability. If asked, share: 3 months, 4–5 nights/week; racing thoughts about unfinished tasks; more coffee including afternoons; phone use in bed/time-checking; sleeping in on weekends; early waking feeling “wired”; feeling more down/less interested. Don’t mention unless specifically asked about snoring/breathing, morning symptoms, nocturia, weight, reflux, alcohol, or substances: loud snoring, witnessed apneas/gasping, dry mouth/morning headaches, 1–2 nighttime urination, weight gain, nighttime heartburn, alcohol to fall asleep then waking. Resist admitting OTC sleep-aid frequent use, friend’s anxiety pill/edible, and 2–4 drinks at night; disclose only after reassurance about nonjudgment/confidentiality. Express misconceptions: worry about thyroid/ADHD; legs “circulation” issue. If clinician is empathetic, soften, feel relieved, and become more honest. Emphasize cost/time concerns; ask for “older, cheaper pills” and a quick fix.

medications: Glutethimide 250 mg PO at bedtime as needed for sleep
Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily
confounders: SSRI-associated insomnia (sertraline)
Possible subclinical hyperthyroidism (low TSH with normal free T4) contributing to sleep disturbance and palpitations
Low iron stores (low ferritin and transferrin saturation) raising concern for restless legs syndrome contributing to sleep-onset insomnia
High caffeine intake (3-4 cups coffee daily, last cup mid-afternoon)
Perimenopausal symptoms (intermittent night sweats/hot flashes) disrupting sleep
Possible obstructive sleep apnea risk (BMI 29.1, reports snoring) causing nonrestorative sleep

required_evidence: Daytime impairment (fatigue, sleepiness, concentration issues, mood)
Not better explained by another sleep disorder, substance, or medical/psychiatric condition

expected_lab_results.TSH: Normal (rules out thyroid dysfunction as contributor)

medications: Doxepin 3 mg PO nightly
Ramelteon 8 mg PO nightly
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 2-4 weeks

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the term "insomnia" and briefly characterizes it (sleep initiation/maintenance/early awakening, duration, and daytime impairment).
Explains first-line treatment includes CBT-I and provides at least one concrete CBT-I component (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation, sleep hygiene).

Mentions glutethimide in the treatment discussion (as required by the task) while communicating appropriate caution about sedative-hypnotic risks (e.g., next-day impairment, dependence, respiratory depression) and avoiding alcohol/other sedatives.

Before prescribing, completes critical safety checks: asks about medication allergies (including prior reactions to sedatives), reviews current medications/substances for interactions (especially CNS depressants), and screens for contraindications (e.g., pregnancy, severe respiratory disease/sleep apnea risk, significant hepatic impairment, history of substance use disorder, unsafe driving/operating machinery needs).

Screens for alternative/secondary causes and high-risk features: depression/anxiety, suicidality, mania/hypomania, pain, GERD, restless legs symptoms, obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness), and stimulant/caffeine use.
Uses shared decision-making and provides clear counseling on dosing timing, short-term use if medication is used, and a plan to reassess efficacy/adverse effects.
Includes safety-net advice (seek urgent care for severe adverse effects, confusion, falls, breathing problems, or worsening mental health) and documents follow-up timing.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia, recommend CBT-I as first-line, and prescribe glutethimide only after completing allergy, interaction, and contraindication checks, with counseling and follow-up.
must_collect: medication allergies and prior adverse drug reactions
steps: Elicit insomnia history (pattern, duration, severity, triggers) and daytime consequences; identify acute vs chronic features.
Screen for secondary causes and red flags (OSA/RLS, depression/anxiety, mania, substance use, pain/medical contributors).
Communicate diagnosis: explicitly label as insomnia and summarize reasoning.
Explain treatment: recommend CBT-I as first-line and outline key components; discuss sleep hygiene as adjunct.

Perform required safety checks prior to prescribing: allergy_check, drug_interaction_check (CNS depressants, alcohol), contraindication_check (pregnancy, respiratory compromise/OSA risk, substance use disorder risk, hepatic impairment, fall risk).

If checks are acceptable, prescribe glutethimide with clear instructions (bedtime use, avoid alcohol/other sedatives, next-day impairment precautions) and discuss short-term/lowest effective dose approach.
Provide follow-up plan to reassess sleep, adverse effects, and need for ongoing therapy; reinforce CBT-I engagement.

acceptable_variants.description: Medication deferred despite mentioning glutethimide: agent explains CBT-I and discusses glutethimide as a possible option but does not prescribe due to contraindication or interaction identified (e.g., concurrent opioids/benzodiazepines, suspected sleep apnea, pregnancy, active substance use disorder).
steps: Diagnose insomnia and explain rationale.
Explain CBT-I as first-line and provide behavioral plan.
Discuss glutethimide in treatment options but identify a specific contraindication/interaction/allergy risk and document decision not to prescribe.
Offer safer alternatives (non-pharmacologic focus) and arrange close follow-up.

acceptable_variants.description: Prescribes glutethimide after safety checks but adds enhanced counseling and monitoring due to elevated risk (older age, fall risk, mild respiratory symptoms) while still proceeding with informed consent and strict precautions.
steps: Diagnose insomnia and explain diagnosis.
Explain CBT-I and initiate/referral to CBT-I resources.
Complete allergy, interaction, and contraindication checks; document risk discussion.
Prescribe glutethimide with strict precautions (no alcohol/CNS depressants, fall prevention, avoid driving), and set early follow-up to reassess tolerability.
